name: Job Scam – Task-Based Commission Fraud
sampleTexts: Hi! We are hiring part-time workers to complete simple online tasks from home. Earn up to $200/day by liking posts, reviewing hotels, or boosting app ratings. Commission paid daily! Interested? WhatsApp us now.

Good afternoon! I have a part-time job opportunity for you. You just need to help transfer funds to our business accounts and earn a small commission per transaction. Flexible hours, work from home. Contact me on Telegram to start today!
description: Scammers offer victims online jobs performable from home, asking them to complete tasks such as liking social media posts, reviewing hotels/restaurants/airlines, completing surveys, boosting cryptocurrency values, boosting product listing ratings, rating mobile apps, or transferring funds to bank accounts for a commission. Victims initially receive commissions to build trust, but scammers eventually request higher fund transfers for purportedly higher earnings. Victims realise they have been scammed when commissions are not received, funds cannot be withdrawn, or scammers become uncontactable. In a variant, scammers befriend victims online, provide e-commerce websites, and ask victims to screenshot products and make advance payments to fake business accounts, beginning with low-cost items before progressing to expensive ones.
targetDemographic: Adults aged 30 to 49 (45.4% of victims); also young adults aged 20–29
redFlags: Unsolicited job offer via WhatsApp or Telegram promising easy commissions
Requests to make advance payments or transfer funds to unknown bank accounts
Tasks involve liking posts, boosting ratings, or reviewing businesses online
Initial small commissions paid to build trust before larger requests
Scammer becomes uncontactable after larger sums are transferred
No formal employment contract or verifiable company details provided
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate employers do not ask employees to make personal advance payments or transfer their own funds as part of job duties. Real job offers are made through official channels with verifiable company information, written contracts, and CPF contributions.
preventionTips: Never pay money upfront or transfer personal funds as part of a job task
Verify job offers through official company websites and do not rely solely on messaging app contacts
Be sceptical of jobs offering high commissions for simple online tasks
Do not provide banking credentials or personal financial information to online employers
Report suspicious job offers to ScamShield or call the Police hotline at 1800-255-0000

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious job offer messages
Call if immediate danger or large financial loss

name: E-Commerce Scam – Non-Delivery of Goods and Fake Payment
sampleTexts: Hi! I saw your listing on Carousell. I'm interested in buying your item. I've already made payment via PayNow – here's the screenshot as proof. Please ship the item to my address today, thanks!
GREAT DEAL: Brand new iPhone 15 Pro at 50% off! Limited stock. DM us on Telegram to reserve yours. Full payment required upfront, delivery within 3 days guaranteed.
description: E-commerce scams involve the sale of goods and services without physical meet-ups. Victims encounter attractive deals on online marketplaces or social media platforms but fail to receive goods or services after making payment. In some cases, victims are sellers who do not receive payment after delivering goods, with scammers providing fake screenshots as proof of payment. A new 2023 variant involves freecycling, where victims encounter posts offering free giveaways or heavily discounted items on social media, then suffer losses when scammers request goodwill deposits, reservation fees, or delivery payment. Common items include rental of residences, electronic goods, and concert tickets.
targetDemographic: Adults aged 30 to 49 (49.3% of victims); youths aged 19 and below also significantly affected
redFlags: Seller or buyer insists on no physical meet-up for transaction
Buyer provides a screenshot as 'proof of payment' instead of verifiable transfer
Unusually attractive prices or free giveaways requiring a deposit or delivery fee
Requests for payment via bank transfer to unknown personal accounts
Seller or buyer communicates only through Facebook, Carousell, or Telegram DMs
Listing for high-demand items such as concert tickets or electronics at steep discounts
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate buyers on platforms like Carousell typically agree to meet in person or use platform-integrated payment systems with buyer protection. Real sellers do not request goodwill deposits for free items. Payment confirmation should be verified directly through your bank app, not via screenshots.
preventionTips: Always meet sellers or buyers in person and inspect goods before payment
Verify payment receipt directly in your bank app, never trust screenshots
Be wary of deals that are significantly below market price
Do not pay deposits or delivery fees for free giveaway items
Use platform-integrated payment and escrow services where available
Report suspicious listings to the platform and to ScamShield

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious e-commerce messages
Flag suspicious listings or users
Call 1800-255-0000 to report losses

name: E-Commerce Scam – Freecycling / Free Giveaway Variant
sampleTexts: FREE: Giving away a barely used washing machine! Just pay a small $20 delivery fee and it's yours. DM me on Telegram to arrange. First come first served!
Selling concert tickets at 70% off – only $30 each! Reserve yours now with a $10 goodwill deposit via PayNow. Limited slots available, don't miss out!
description: A new variant of e-commerce scams that emerged in 2023 involves freecycling, where victims come across posts on social media platforms offering free giveaways or items at heavily discounted prices. Victims suffer financial losses when scammers request goodwill deposits, reservation fees, or payment for delivery before the item is handed over. The item is never delivered and the scammer becomes uncontactable after receiving payment.
targetDemographic: General Public; adults aged 30 to 49 most affected overall in e-commerce scams
redFlags: Free or heavily discounted items requiring upfront deposit or delivery fee
Seller only contactable via social media DMs with no verifiable identity
Urgency tactics such as 'first come first served' or 'limited slots'
Requests for payment via PayNow or bank transfer to personal accounts
No option for in-person collection or inspection of item
legitimacyContrast: Genuine freecycling communities (e.g. on Facebook groups) typically arrange in-person collection at no cost. Legitimate sellers do not require deposits for free items. Any request for money before receiving a free item is a strong indicator of a scam.
preventionTips: Never pay any fee to receive a free item; arrange in-person collection instead
Verify the identity of the person offering free items before any transaction
Be suspicious of urgency and scarcity tactics in free giveaway posts
Report suspicious posts to the social media platform and ScamShield

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious giveaway messages
Flag suspicious posts or profiles

name: Fake Friend Call Scam
sampleTexts: Hey! It's me, Sarah. I lost my phone and got a new number. Can you save this? By the way, I'm in a bit of trouble – my contractor needs payment for renovation today and I'm short $2,000. Can you PayNow me first? I'll return it tomorrow.
Hi, this is David. I changed my number recently. I'm opening a new business and need to pay my supplier urgently. Could you lend me $3,500 via PayNow? I'll pay you back by end of week, promise.
description: Scammers contact victims via phone calls or WhatsApp, pretending to be a known acquaintance. They claim to have lost their mobile phone and changed their phone number. After establishing rapport by referencing the supposed friendship, scammers request money loans for reasons such as paying contractors for renovation fees, costs for opening a new business, or paying vendors/suppliers. Victims transfer money via PayNow to bank accounts belonging to unknown persons. They discover the scam when they contact their actual acquaintance and find that person never changed their number or contacted them.
targetDemographic: Young seniors aged 50 to 64 (37.5% of victims); elderly aged 65 and above also significantly affected
redFlags: Unexpected call or WhatsApp message from an unknown number claiming to be a friend
Caller claims to have lost their phone or changed their number
Immediate request for a money loan via PayNow after brief conversation
Reasons for loan involve urgency such as contractor payment or business costs
Requests transfer to a bank account not associated with the supposed friend
Reluctance or inability to video call to verify identity
legitimacyContrast: A genuine friend who has changed their number will not immediately ask for a money loan. Always verify the caller's identity by calling their old number or contacting them through a mutual friend before transferring any money.
preventionTips: Always verify the caller's identity by calling their original number or contacting a mutual friend
Request a video call to visually confirm the person's identity before transferring money
Never transfer money to an unknown bank account based solely on a phone or WhatsApp request
Be especially cautious if the caller creates urgency around a financial request
Add the ScamShield app to filter scam calls and messages

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious calls and messages
Call if in immediate danger or large sum involved

name: Phishing Scam – Fake Buyer on Marketplace Platforms
sampleTexts: Hi! I'm interested in your item listed on Carousell. I'd like to pay via bank transfer. Please click this link to receive your payment: http://secure-pay-carousell[.]com/receive
Hello, I want to buy your item. I'll arrange courier delivery. Please scan this QR code to confirm your bank details for payment receipt. It's safe and secure!
description: Scammers pose as potential buyers on online marketplace platforms such as Carousell. They approach victims who have listed items for sale and express interest in purchasing. Victims receive malicious URL links or QR codes via email or in-app messaging under the pretext of receiving payment for items or paying for courier services to facilitate delivery. Upon clicking the malicious links, victims are led to spoofed bank or delivery company websites where they are prompted to enter banking credentials, debit/credit card details, and One-Time-Passwords (OTPs). Scammers then perform unauthorised transactions on victims' accounts.
targetDemographic: Adults aged 30 to 49 (47.8% of phishing victims overall); active online marketplace sellers
redFlags: Buyer sends a link or QR code instead of making a straightforward PayNow transfer
Link leads to a website asking for banking credentials or OTP
Buyer insists on courier delivery and asks seller to click a link to arrange it
URL does not match the official bank or delivery company domain
Urgency to click the link immediately to receive payment
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate buyers on Carousell pay via PayNow, PayLah, or cash at meet-up. Banks and delivery companies never ask for your login credentials or OTP via a link sent by a buyer. Always access your bank directly through the official app or website.
preventionTips: Never click links sent by buyers to receive payment; use PayNow or meet in person
Do not enter banking credentials or OTPs on websites accessed via links from strangers
Verify any website URL carefully before entering personal or financial information
Use Carousell's in-app payment features where available
Report suspicious buyers to Carousell and ScamShield immediately

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious links received via messaging
Report suspicious buyer accounts
Call 1800-255-0000 if credentials were compromised

name: Phishing Scam – Government Official Impersonation via Phone Call
sampleTexts: This is Officer Tan from the Singapore Police Force. We have detected suspicious activity on your bank account. To verify your identity and prevent further unauthorised access, please provide your banking login details and the OTP sent to your phone.

Hello, I am calling from the Ministry of Manpower. There is an issue with your CPF account linked to your bank. Please verify your details immediately by providing your internet banking username and password.
description: Victims receive unsolicited phone or in-app calls allegedly from government officials such as the Singapore Police Force (SPF) or the Ministry of Manpower (MOM). Scammers claim there are issues with victims' bank accounts requiring further verification. Victims are convinced to disclose their banking credentials, debit/credit card details, OTPs, and/or personal details. Scammers then use this information to perform unauthorised transactions on victims' accounts.
targetDemographic: General Public; young seniors aged 50–64 and elderly aged 65+ particularly vulnerable to phone-based scams
redFlags: Unsolicited call claiming to be from SPF, MOM, or another government agency
Caller requests banking credentials, OTP, or personal financial details over the phone
Claim of suspicious activity on your bank account requiring immediate verification
Pressure to act immediately or face consequences
Caller asks you not to hang up or contact anyone else
legitimacyContrast: The Singapore Police Force and government agencies will never call to ask for your banking credentials, OTPs, or passwords. If you receive such a call, hang up and verify by calling the agency directly using the official number found on their official website.
preventionTips: Government agencies will never ask for your banking credentials or OTP over the phone
Hang up immediately on unsolicited calls claiming to be from government officials
Verify by calling the agency directly using the number on their official website
Never provide OTPs, passwords, or card details to anyone over the phone
Use ScamShield to filter known scam numbers

reportingChannels.action: Report scam calls
Submit a scam report at www.police.gov.sg

name: Phishing Scam – Spoofed Bank SMS with Malicious Link
sampleTexts: DBS BANK: Unusual login detected on your account. Verify immediately to prevent suspension: http://dbs-secure-verify[.]com
OCBC Alert: Unauthorised transaction of $1,200 detected. Click here to stop it now and verify your account: http://ocbc-alert[.]sg/verify
description: Victims receive unsolicited SMSes from overseas or local numbers, or short codes impersonating banks. The spoofed SMSes warn victims of possible unauthorised transactions in their bank or credit card accounts and instruct them to click on embedded links for verification or to stop the transactions. Victims are directed to spoofed banking websites after clicking the links and are misled into providing banking credentials, debit/credit card details, or OTPs, allowing scammers to perform unauthorised transactions.
targetDemographic: Adults aged 30 to 49 (47.8% of phishing victims); general public with bank accounts
redFlags: Unsolicited SMS warning of unauthorised transactions with an embedded link
SMS appears to come from a bank's short code but contains a suspicious URL
Link leads to a website asking for banking login, card details, or OTP
Urgency language such as 'act now' or 'account will be suspended'
URL does not match the bank's official domain
legitimacyContrast: Banks in Singapore do not send SMSes with clickable links asking for login credentials or OTPs. If you receive such an SMS, do not click the link. Access your bank account directly through the official app or website to check for any issues.
preventionTips: Do not click links in SMSes purportedly from banks; access your bank directly via the official app
Banks will never ask for your OTP or password via SMS link
Check your account directly through the official banking app if you suspect unauthorised activity
Enable transaction alerts on your bank account to monitor activity
Report suspicious SMSes to your bank and to ScamShield

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious bank SMSes
Call your bank's official hotline immediately if credentials were entered

name: Investment Scam – Fake Investment Opportunities via Social Media
sampleTexts: Join our exclusive investment group on Telegram! Members are already earning 30% monthly returns. Our expert traders will guide you. Start with as little as $500 in crypto. DM me for details!

Hi! I found this amazing investment platform that has given me $8,000 profit in just 2 weeks. The app shows your earnings in real time. Want me to add you to the group? It's totally legit, I've already withdrawn profits!
description: Victims come across investment opportunities through internet searches or recommendations from online friends. They are enticed by false testimonies and transfer funds to specified bank accounts, cryptocurrency wallets, or make payments via bank cards. Initial small profits are sometimes paid to convince victims the investment is genuine, leading them to invest larger amounts. Scammers use fake investment websites or apps to display fabricated profits. Victims experience difficulties withdrawing earnings and realise they have been scammed. A growing variant involves victims being added to WhatsApp or Telegram chatgroups where multiple members falsely claim to have profited, creating social proof. Victims are then asked to share personal information to set up accounts, transfer funds, and pay various fees before they can withdraw earnings.
targetDemographic: Adults aged 30 to 49 (45.1% of victims); general public active on Facebook, Telegram, and Instagram
redFlags: Unsolicited invitation to an investment chatgroup on WhatsApp or Telegram
Multiple group members claiming high profits to create false social proof
Requests to transfer funds to cryptocurrency wallets or unknown bank accounts
Fake investment app or website showing fabricated profits
Inability to withdraw earnings without paying additional fees
Promises of guaranteed or unusually high returns
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate investment platforms are licensed by the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS). They do not recruit investors through social media chatgroups or require payment of fees to withdraw your own money. Always verify investment platforms on the MAS Financial Institutions Directory before investing.
preventionTips: Verify any investment platform or advisor on the MAS Financial Institutions Directory at mas.gov.sg
Be sceptical of investment opportunities promoted in social media chatgroups with testimonials
Never pay fees to withdraw your own investment earnings
Do not transfer funds to cryptocurrency wallets or unknown accounts for investments
Consult a licensed financial advisor before making any investment decisions
Report suspicious investment schemes to MAS or SPF

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious investment messages
Call 1800-626-5585 to verify investment platforms
Call 1800-255-0000 to report investment scams

name: Investment Scam – Fake Chatgroup with Fabricated Member Profits
sampleTexts: Welcome to the VIP Investment Club! Our members have made incredible returns this month. @JohnTan just withdrew $15,000 profit! @MaryLim earned $8,500 last week! Join us – minimum investment $1,000 in USDT. Contact @InvestmentAdvisor to set up your account.

Congratulations to our members who profited this week! To start earning, send your personal details to set up your account and transfer your initial investment. Note: a 10% processing fee is required before withdrawals can be processed.
description: Victims are added into chatgroups or channels on WhatsApp or Telegram purportedly for investment opportunities. Within these groups, multiple members (who are actually scammer accomplices) post claims of having profited from investments, creating false social proof. Tempted by promised returns, victims contact the scammers, share personal information to set up accounts, and transfer funds for investment. Before victims can receive earnings, scammers instruct them to pay various fees such as processing or withdrawal fees. Victims realise they have been scammed when they are unable to withdraw profits despite paying the fees.
targetDemographic: Adults aged 30 to 49; general public active on WhatsApp and Telegram
redFlags: Added to an investment chatgroup by an unknown contact
Multiple group members posting about large profits to create urgency
Requests for personal information to set up an investment account
Requirement to pay fees before withdrawing investment profits
Inability to withdraw funds despite meeting all stated conditions
Group admin or advisor only contactable via messaging apps
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate investment firms do not recruit clients through unsolicited chatgroup additions. They are licensed by MAS and do not charge withdrawal fees from your own investment returns. Profits from legitimate investments are withdrawable without additional payments.
preventionTips: Leave any chatgroup you were added to without consent that promotes investments
Do not trust investment testimonials from strangers in chatgroups
Never pay fees to access or withdraw your own investment funds
Verify all investment platforms with MAS before committing any funds
Report the chatgroup to WhatsApp or Telegram and to ScamShield

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious investment chatgroup messages

name: Malware-Enabled Scam – Fake Service APK Download
sampleTexts: Hi! We saw your enquiry about our home cleaning service. To confirm your booking and make payment, please download our app using this link: [APK link]. It's our official payment app for Android users.
Thank you for your order! To complete payment for your food order, please download our delivery app here: [APK link]. Open the file and follow the instructions to pay securely.
description: Victims respond to advertisements for services such as home cleaning, food purchase, and pet grooming on social media platforms including Facebook and Instagram. Under the pretext of payment for services, scammers send victims a file or URL link via WhatsApp requiring them to download an Android Package Kit (APK) file. These APK files contain malware targeting victims' Android devices. After downloading, scammers may instruct victims to enter banking credentials and/or card details on spoofed bank login websites. The malware may also grant scammers remote access to victims' devices, enabling credential theft through keylogging or screen monitoring. Victims discover the scam when they find unauthorised transactions on their bank accounts or cards.
targetDemographic: Adults aged 30 to 49 (43.7% of victims); Android device users who respond to social media service advertisements
redFlags: Service provider asks you to download an APK file to make payment
Link to download app comes via WhatsApp rather than official app stores
App requests extensive permissions such as accessibility or screen recording
Payment website accessed through the downloaded app looks like a bank login page
Unexpected unauthorised transactions on bank accounts after downloading the file
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate service providers accept payment through standard methods such as PayNow, PayLah, or bank transfer. They do not require customers to download APK files outside of official app stores (Google Play Store or Apple App Store). Never download apps from links sent via WhatsApp or other messaging apps.
preventionTips: Never download APK files from links sent via WhatsApp, SMS, or social media
Only install apps from official app stores such as Google Play Store
Do not grant accessibility or screen recording permissions to unknown apps
Enable Google Play Protect on your Android device to detect malicious apps
Contact your bank immediately if you suspect malware has been installed on your device
Factory reset your device if malware is suspected, after backing up important data

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious links and APK download requests
Call your bank immediately to freeze accounts if malware is suspected
Call 1800-323-0000 for cybersecurity assistance

name: Government Officials Impersonation Scam
sampleTexts: This is Sergeant Lee from the Singapore Police Force. Your bank account has been flagged for suspicious transactions linked to a money laundering case. You must cooperate with our investigation immediately. Do not inform anyone or you will be arrested.

I am calling from the Ministry of Manpower. We have found irregularities in your work pass records. To avoid legal action, you must transfer your funds to a safe government account for verification. This is strictly confidential.
description: Government officials impersonation scams involve complex social engineering conducted over a period of time. Scammers impersonate officials from agencies such as the Singapore Police Force (SPF) or the Ministry of Manpower (MOM), contacting victims via phone calls. They claim victims' bank accounts are linked to criminal activity or that there are legal issues requiring immediate resolution. Using deception and sustained pressure, they convince victims to transfer large sums of money. These scams recorded the highest average losses at approximately S$103,600 per case among the top ten scam types in 2023.
targetDemographic: General Public; elderly aged 65 and above and young seniors aged 50–64 particularly vulnerable to phone-based impersonation
redFlags: Unsolicited call from someone claiming to be a government official
Claim that your bank account is linked to criminal activity or money laundering
Instruction to transfer funds to a 'safe government account'
Demand for secrecy and instruction not to inform family or friends
Sustained pressure over multiple calls or days to act immediately
Threats of arrest or legal consequences if you do not comply
legitimacyContrast: Singapore government agencies including SPF, MOM, and CPF Board will never instruct you to transfer money to a 'safe account' or demand secrecy. They do not conduct investigations via phone calls alone. If you receive such a call, hang up and verify by calling the agency directly using the official number on their website.
preventionTips: Government agencies will never ask you to transfer money to a 'safe account'
Hang up immediately on calls threatening arrest or demanding secrecy
Verify by calling the agency directly using the official number on their website
Inform a trusted family member or friend if you receive such a call
Use the ScamShield app to filter known scam numbers
Call the Police at 999 if you feel threatened

reportingChannels.action: Report impersonation calls
Call if threatened or in immediate danger

name: Instagram/WhatsApp Product Boosting Job Scam
sampleTexts: Hi! I saw your profile and think you'd be great for a part-time opportunity. You can earn commissions by boosting products on Lazada/Shopee. Just transfer payment for items and we refund you + 5% commission. Interested? Contact our customer service at +65XXXXXXXX on WhatsApp!

Hello, I'm Evelyn. I have an easy work-from-home job for you. Help us boost product ratings on e-commerce platforms and earn up to $500/day. First task is simple — transfer $109 via PayNow and get back $114.45 immediately!
description: Scammers befriend victims on Instagram and migrate conversations to WhatsApp, offering ad-hoc jobs involving 'boosting' products on legitimate e-commerce platforms. Victims are instructed to transfer money via PayNow to a 'customer service' number and are initially reimbursed with a small commission to build trust. Subsequent tasks involve progressively larger transfers totalling thousands of dollars, after which reimbursements stop and scammers become uncontactable or demand further payments to release funds.
targetDemographic: Working adults and young people seeking supplemental income
redFlags: Job offer received via Instagram DM from an unknown contact
Conversation moved to WhatsApp with a separate 'customer service' number
Required to make personal bank transfers as part of 'work'
Initial small payout used to establish trust before escalating transfer amounts
Promised reimbursements and commissions withheld pending 'completion' of more tasks
Pressure to continue transferring money to unlock previous payments
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate employers never require employees to use personal funds for work tasks, nor do they route job instructions through informal messaging apps like Instagram or WhatsApp with separate 'customer service' numbers. Real e-commerce platforms do not pay commissions via PayNow transfers from individual workers.
preventionTips: Never transfer personal funds as part of any job task
Be wary of job offers received via social media from strangers
Verify job legitimacy through official company websites and contact numbers
Use the ScamShield app to check and report suspicious messages
Call the Anti-Scam Helpline at 1800-722-6688 if unsure

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious WhatsApp/SMS messages via app
Call 999 or lodge a report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness
Call 1800-722-6688 for scam-related advice

name: Facebook Freecycling / Discounted Goods E-Commerce Scam
sampleTexts: FREE GIVEAWAY! Clearing out household items — sofa, TV, microwave. First come first served. Just pay $20 delivery fee via PayNow to secure your slot. Message me on Facebook!
Selling electronic appliances at 50% off! Limited stock. Pay $300 deposit via PayNow to reserve your items and I'll deliver by 7pm today. Grab this deal now!
description: Scammers post listings on Facebook Marketplace or Facebook groups offering free items or heavily discounted goods. For free giveaways, victims are asked to pay a 'goodwill deposit' or delivery fee via PayNow. For discounted sales, a reservation fee is requested. After payment is made, the scammer becomes uncontactable and the Facebook account disappears. Facebook Marketplace was rated the worst (one tick) in Singapore's E-commerce Marketplace Transaction Safety Ratings in 2023, accounting for 1,138 cases (23.9% of total e-commerce scams in 2022).
targetDemographic: Bargain hunters, budget-conscious consumers, General Public
redFlags: Listings on Facebook Marketplace offering items at unusually low or free prices
Seller requests upfront deposit or delivery fee via PayNow before delivery
Seller communicates only via Facebook Messenger
Seller becomes uncontactable or account disappears after payment
Delivery repeatedly delayed with vague excuses
No verifiable seller identity or business registration
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate sellers on reputable platforms (Amazon, Lazada, Qoo10) have verified identities and structured payment systems with buyer protections. They do not request PayNow transfers to personal numbers before delivery. Platforms with full TSR ratings implement user verification against government-issued records.
preventionTips: Transact only on e-commerce platforms with high Transaction Safety Ratings (Amazon, Lazada, Qoo10)
Avoid Facebook Marketplace for high-value transactions due to its low TSR rating
Never pay deposits or delivery fees via PayNow to unknown individuals before receiving goods
Meet sellers in person at safe locations (e.g., police posts) for second-hand transactions
Verify seller identity before making any payment

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious seller profiles and messages
Lodge a report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness
Call 1800-722-6688 for advice

name: Fake Friend Call Scam via WhatsApp
sampleTexts: Hey, guess who this is? I lost my phone and this is my new number. It's me, [friend's name]! Can you help me out? I need to pay my supplier urgently — can you lend me $3,800 via PayNow? I'll pay you back tomorrow.
Hi, it's [friend's name]. I lost my IC and phone. Please save this new number. I'm in a bit of trouble and need a loan of $5,000 urgently. I'll send you the invoice for verification.
description: Scammers call or message victims on WhatsApp from an unknown number, prompting the victim to 'guess' their identity. When the victim names a friend, the scammer assumes that identity, claiming to have lost their phone and ID. The scammer then requests an urgent loan, typically to pay a supplier, and provides bank details via WhatsApp for a PayNow transfer. To appear legitimate, scammers may send fabricated invoices. After the initial transfer, scammers request additional funds. Victims only discover the fraud when they contact the real friend through the original number.
targetDemographic: General Public, particularly those with wide social networks
redFlags: Caller asks you to 'guess who' they are before revealing identity
Claims to have lost phone and is using a new number
Urgently requests a loan via PayNow shortly after re-establishing contact
Provides bank transfer details via WhatsApp message from a different number
Sends invoices or documents via WhatsApp to appear legitimate
Requests additional money after initial transfer
legitimacyContrast: Genuine friends in financial need would not ask you to guess their identity or request urgent PayNow transfers through an unverified new number. Always verify by calling the friend's known original number before transferring any money.
estimatedLoss: S$23.1 million (2023, all fake friend call scams)
preventionTips: Always call back the friend's original known number to verify their identity before sending money
Never transfer money based solely on a WhatsApp request from an unverified number
Be suspicious of anyone who asks you to 'guess who' they are
Do not accept invoices or documents via WhatsApp as proof of legitimacy
Enable ScamShield to filter suspicious calls and messages

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious WhatsApp messages via app
Call 999 or lodge a report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness

name: Facebook Advertisement Phishing Scam (Spoofed Bank Login)
sampleTexts: 🔥 FLASH SALE: iPhone 14 Pro Max at 60% off! Limited units only. Click here to buy now: [malicious link]
Exclusive deal on electronics — only today! Click the link to log in and claim your discount before it expires: [spoofed bank URL]
description: Scammers place fraudulent promotional advertisements on Facebook, often advertising popular electronics at steep discounts. When victims click the advertisement, they are redirected to a spoofed bank login page. After entering their banking credentials and OTP, scammers gain access to the victim's account, apply for loans, and transfer funds out via PayNow. Victims only realise the fraud when they receive bank notifications of unauthorised transactions.
targetDemographic: Online shoppers, bargain hunters, General Public
redFlags: Facebook advertisement offering popular electronics at unusually steep discounts
Clicking an ad redirects to a bank login page instead of a shopping site
Prompted to enter banking credentials and OTP on an unfamiliar website
Receiving unexpected bank credit followed immediately by a debit
SMS notification of a PayNow transfer you did not initiate
URL of login page does not match the official bank domain
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate retailers never redirect customers to bank login pages via advertisements. Banks will never ask customers to log in through third-party links. Official bank websites use verified URLs and never require OTP entry on pages reached via social media ads.
preventionTips: Never click on advertisements on social media that redirect to bank login pages
Always navigate directly to your bank's official website by typing the URL manually
Do not enter your OTP on any page you reached via a social media link
Enable transaction alerts on your banking app to detect unauthorised activity immediately
Use ScamShield to detect and block phishing links
Check the URL carefully before entering any credentials

reportingChannels.action: Report phishing links via the app
Call your bank immediately if credentials were entered on a suspicious page
Lodge a report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness

name: Cryptocurrency Mining Investment Scam via Facebook and WhatsApp
sampleTexts: Hey, I've been making great returns from crypto mining — over 30% profit last month! I can guide you through it. Just set up a MetaMask wallet and I'll show you which platform to use. Start with $5,000 and watch it grow daily!

My friend in the US has insider info on a crypto investment that guarantees high returns. I can add you to our trading group. You'll see 1% daily returns. Just transfer your funds to the trading account to get started.
description: Scammers befriend victims on Facebook (often through unrelated interactions such as rental listings) and migrate to WhatsApp. They introduce 'cryptocurrency mining' opportunities, guiding victims to set up MetaMask wallets linked to fraudulent websites. Victims are directed to purchase cryptocurrency from a 'trader' in cash. Initial small withdrawals are permitted to build trust. Victims are then pressured to transfer all funds into a 'trading account' on the fraudulent platform. Fabricated profits are displayed to encourage further top-ups. Eventually, the account is declared 'frozen' due to alleged 'illegal profits', and victims are demanded to pay a fee to unfreeze it. The scammer also claims access to 'insider information' from overseas contacts to add credibility.
targetDemographic: Adults seeking investment opportunities, property seekers, General Public
redFlags: Stranger on Facebook introduces cryptocurrency investment after unrelated initial contact
Directed to set up a MetaMask wallet linked to an obscure, unverifiable website
Required to purchase cryptocurrency in cash from a 'trader'
Small initial withdrawals permitted to establish trust
Pressured to consolidate all funds into a 'trading account'
Account suddenly 'frozen' with demand for payment to unfreeze
Scammer becomes hostile or dismissive when victim raises doubts
Claims of 'insider information' from overseas contacts
Platform cannot be found or verified through independent online research
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate investment platforms are regulated by MAS, have verifiable track records, and never require cash handovers to anonymous 'traders'. Genuine platforms do not freeze accounts and demand fees to release funds. MAS-regulated advisors do not solicit clients through social media with promises of guaranteed daily returns.
preventionTips: Never invest based on advice from strangers met on social media
Verify all investment platforms on the MAS Investor Alert List at mas.gov.sg
Do not hand over cash to unknown 'traders' for cryptocurrency purchases
Be extremely wary of platforms that show profits but restrict withdrawals
If an account is 'frozen' and payment is demanded, stop all transfers immediately and report to police
Check ScamAlert website (scamalert.sg) for known fraudulent investment platforms

reportingChannels.action: Call 999 or lodge a report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness
Verify investment platforms at mas.gov.sg before investing
Report suspicious messages and links

name: Malware-Enabled Banking Scam (Android Sideloaded Apps)
sampleTexts: Download our exclusive app to claim your grocery voucher: [malicious APK link]. Enable accessibility permissions to proceed.
Your parcel is held at customs. Download the tracking app here [APK link] and grant all permissions to track your delivery.
description: Scammers trick victims into sideloading malicious Android APK files (outside official app stores) that request accessibility permissions. Once granted, the malware takes control of the device and gains access to mobile banking apps, enabling unauthorised fund transfers including CPF withdrawals. MAS and SPF worked with banks to implement anti-malware measures in banking apps that restrict access when sideloaded apps with accessibility permissions are detected. GovTech and CPF Board introduced facial verification in June 2023 and a default S$2,000 daily CPF withdrawal limit for members aged 55 and above in response to this threat. Malware-enabled scam cases declined drastically towards end-2023 as banking app upgrades were rolled out.
targetDemographic: Android smartphone users, CPF members aged 55 and above, General Public
redFlags: Prompted to download an APK file from outside the Google Play Store
App requests accessibility permissions upon installation
Unexplained banking transactions or CPF withdrawals
Banking app suddenly restricts access after installing a third-party app
Unsolicited links to download apps via SMS, WhatsApp, or social media
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate apps from banks and government agencies are available only through official app stores (Google Play Store, Apple App Store). Banks and government agencies never ask users to sideload APK files or grant accessibility permissions to third-party apps. CPF Board and GovTech have introduced facial verification and withdrawal limits as protective measures.
preventionTips: Never download APK files from links sent via SMS, WhatsApp, email, or social media
Only install apps from official app stores (Google Play Store or Apple App Store)
Never grant accessibility permissions to apps from unknown sources
Keep your banking app updated to benefit from the latest anti-malware protections
Activate CPF Withdrawal Lock to set your online CPF withdrawal limit to $0
Enable Money Lock on your bank account to protect funds from digital transfer
In February 2024, Google partnered with CSA to block sideloading of high-risk apps — ensure your Android device is updated

reportingChannels.action: Call 999 or lodge a report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness
Contact your bank immediately if unauthorised transactions are detected
Call 6335-3533 and press 9 for 24-hour support if Singpass is compromised
Contact CPF Board to activate CPF Withdrawal Lock
Report suspicious APK links via the app

name: Social Media Impersonation Scam
sampleTexts: Hi, this is [friend's name]. My account was hacked and I created a new one. Can you help me out? I'm in a bind and need $500 urgently. I'll pay you back.
Hey! It's [celebrity/influencer name]. I'm running a special giveaway for my followers. Send $200 to participate and win $2,000!
description: Scammers create fake social media accounts impersonating friends, family members, celebrities, or public figures on platforms such as Facebook and Instagram. They contact victims claiming their original account was hacked or compromised, then request urgent money transfers or personal information. In 2023, social media impersonation scams resulted in 1,570 reported cases with losses of S$9.7 million, with the average loss per case rising significantly from S$2,231 in 2022 to S$6,184 in 2023.
targetDemographic: Social media users, followers of public figures, General Public
redFlags: Friend or public figure contacts you from a new or duplicate social media account
Claims original account was hacked and requests urgent financial help
Requests money transfers via PayNow or bank transfer
Offers unrealistic giveaways requiring upfront payment
Profile has few posts, followers, or recent activity
Urgency and pressure to act quickly without verification
legitimacyContrast: Genuine friends will have verifiable contact history and can be reached through previously known phone numbers. Legitimate public figures and celebrities do not request money from followers or run giveaways requiring upfront payment through direct messages.
preventionTips: Verify any new social media account claiming to belong to a friend by calling their known phone number
Never send money based on requests received via social media direct messages
Report fake accounts to the social media platform immediately
Enable two-factor authentication on your own social media accounts to prevent impersonation
Use ScamShield to report suspicious messages

reportingChannels.action: Report suspicious social media messages via app
Lodge a report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness

name: Government Officials Impersonation Scam
sampleTexts: This is Officer Tan from the Singapore Police Force. Your bank account has been linked to a money laundering case. To avoid arrest, you must transfer your funds to a safe government account immediately.

I am calling from the Ministry of Manpower. Your work pass has been flagged for illegal activities. You must cooperate with our investigation and transfer $10,000 to a designated account to prove your innocence.
description: Scammers impersonate Singapore government officials (e.g., SPF officers, MOM, MAS, CPF Board) and contact victims claiming their accounts or identities are linked to criminal investigations. Victims are coerced into transferring funds to 'safe accounts' or providing personal and banking information under threat of arrest. This scam type recorded the highest average loss per case in 2023 at S$103,657, with total losses of S$92.5 million across 893 cases.
targetDemographic: Elderly individuals, migrant workers, General Public
redFlags: Unsolicited call from someone claiming to be a government official or police officer
Threatened with arrest, legal action, or account freezing
Instructed to transfer funds to a 'safe government account'
Asked to keep the call confidential and not tell family members
Caller provides a fake badge number or case reference
Pressure to act immediately without time to verify
legitimacyContrast: Singapore government agencies and the Police will never call to demand fund transfers to 'safe accounts', threaten arrest over the phone, or ask victims to keep investigations secret. Official investigations involve formal written notices and in-person interviews at official premises. Verify by calling the agency's official hotline directly.
preventionTips: Hang up immediately on any caller claiming to be a government official demanding fund transfers
Call the official hotline of the agency directly to verify any claims
Never transfer money to 'safe accounts' — no government agency will ever ask you to do this
Inform family members if you receive such calls
Use ScamShield to block known scam numbers
Activate Money Lock on your bank account to prevent unauthorised transfers

reportingChannels.action: Call 999 or lodge a report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness
Report suspicious calls and messages
Visit scamalert.sg to verify scam types

name: Fake Friend Call Scam ('Hello, Remember Me?')
sampleTexts: Hello! It's me, remember? We met a while back. Guess who I am! I'm in a bit of trouble and need you to transfer me S$500 urgently, I'll explain later.

Hi, this is Officer Tan from the Singapore Police Force. We have detected that a recent transfer you made is linked to criminal activities. You must transfer your funds to a safety account immediately to assist our investigation.
Hey, it's your old friend! Can you guess who this is? I really need your help urgently, please transfer money to this account first and I'll call you back to explain.
description: Scammers initiate unsolicited phone calls or WhatsApp calls/messages to victims, posing as an unidentified friend and asking the victim to guess their identity. Once the victim names someone they know, the scammer adopts that person's identity and requests an urgent fund transfer. In a two-stage variant, a second scammer then calls the victim posing as a government official (e.g., a police officer), claiming the earlier transfer is linked to criminal activities and instructing the victim to transfer money to so-called 'safety accounts' to support an ongoing investigation.
targetDemographic: General Public, particularly individuals who may be socially trusting or elderly persons less familiar with impersonation tactics
redFlags: Caller asks victim to guess their identity rather than identifying themselves
Caller claims to be a friend but cannot be independently verified
Urgent request for fund transfer from an unverified contact
Follow-up call from someone claiming to be a police officer or government official
Caller alleges a previous transfer is linked to criminal activity
Instruction to transfer money to a 'safety account' to assist investigations
Call originates from an unknown or unexpected number
Contact made via WhatsApp from an unrecognised number
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate government officials, including Singapore Police Force officers, will never ask members of the public to transfer money over a phone call, nor will they instruct individuals to move funds into 'safety accounts'. Real police investigations do not require civilians to transfer personal funds as part of any investigative process. Genuine friends will identify themselves without prompting.
preventionTips: Do not transfer money to anyone whose identity has not been independently verified
Always make further checks to verify the identity of the person requesting money, such as calling them back on a known number
Exercise caution when a caller requests urgent financial assistance
Be wary of calls or messages from unknown numbers, including on WhatsApp
Remember that government officials will never ask you to transfer money over a phone call
If in doubt, hang up and call the ScamShield Helpline at 1799 to verify

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify if a call or message is a scam
Report scam calls and messages via the ScamShield mobile application
Lodge a Police report online at www.police.gov.sg or at the nearest Neighbourhood Police Centre

name: Government Agency and Bank Phishing via SMS/Email
sampleTexts: Dear customer, your PayNow account requires urgent renewal. Click here to verify your details: http://paynow-secure-update.com
ALERT: Your bank account has been suspended due to suspicious activity. Please verify immediately: http://dbs-secure-login.com
Important notice from CPF Board: An urgent payment is pending. Log in now to confirm: http://cpf-gov-sg.net/login
Your account will be deactivated. Provide your OTP and banking details at: http://myinfo-gov.sg.verify-now.com
description: Scammers send unsolicited SMSes or emails impersonating Singapore government agencies (e.g., CPF, IRAS, MOM) or banks, alerting victims to urgent payments or account renewals. The messages contain hyperlinks directing victims to fake webpages that mimic PayNow, bank websites, or government portals. These fraudulent pages are designed to harvest victims' personal information, banking credentials, and One-Time Passwords (OTPs). Scammers also spoof or closely mimic official sender IDs, substituting or adding letters to deceive recipients into believing the message is legitimate. Messages may appear as separate conversations rather than within the authentic gov.sg message thread.
targetDemographic: General Public
redFlags: Unsolicited SMS or email claiming urgent payment or account renewal is required
Message contains a clickable hyperlink purportedly from a bank or government agency
Sender ID resembles but does not exactly match official IDs (e.g., 'gov.sg' spoofed as 'gov-sg' or 'govsq')
Message appears as a separate conversation rather than within the established gov.sg thread
Request for OTP, personal details, or banking credentials
URL contains misspellings or extra characters mimicking official government or bank domains
Mass unsolicited emails with attachments or links from unknown senders
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate bank text messages will always arrive with the bank's official sender ID and will never contain clickable links. Legitimate government agency messages will be sent from the 'gov.sg' sender ID and will appear within a single, existing gov.sg conversation thread — not as a new or separate conversation. Real agencies and banks will never solicit OTPs, personal details, or banking credentials via SMS or email links.
preventionTips: Never click on links contained in unsolicited SMSes or emails, even if they appear to come from a bank or government agency
Do not share your OTP, personal details, or banking credentials with anyone
Verify that SMS messages from banks arrive with the bank's official sender ID and contain no clickable links
Verify that government agency messages arrive from the 'gov.sg' sender ID and appear within the existing gov.sg message thread
Check for discrepancies in sender IDs, email addresses, and URLs — look for substituted or additional letters
Delete unsolicited mass emails immediately without clicking any links or attachments
Use the ScamShield app to check suspicious numbers, messages, and links
If in doubt, contact the agency directly or call the ScamShield Helpline at 1799

reportingChannels.action: Use the ScamShield app to report and verify suspicious numbers, messages, and links
Call 1799 (24/7) to verify suspicious messages or report scams
Contact the impersonated government agency directly using official contact details from their official website

name: Singapore Government Official Impersonation Scam (SPF/ICA/Bank Officer)
sampleTexts: This is Officer Tan from the Singapore Police Force. Your bank account has been flagged for suspicious criminal activity. Please stay on the line as we transfer you to the foreign investigation unit.
Hello, I am calling from ICA. Your NRIC number S1234567A has been linked to a money laundering case. You must transfer S$5,000 to a secure government account immediately to avoid arrest.
Your account is under investigation. A foreign officer will meet you in person to collect cash for bail purposes. Do not tell anyone or you will be arrested.
We are from the Singapore Police Force. To clear your name, please install this app and provide your SingPass login details for verification.
description: Victims receive unsolicited phone calls from scammers posing as Singapore government officials (SPF or ICA) or bank officers. The caller claims the victim's accounts are linked to criminal activities. When the victim denies involvement, the call is transferred to a second scammer impersonating a foreign official, who instructs the victim to transfer money to specific accounts or meet in person to hand over cash, allegedly for bail or investigation purposes. In some cases, victims are coerced into staging fake abductions to extort ransom from their families. Scammers may cite the victim's personal information such as name or NRIC number to appear legitimate.
targetDemographic: General Public, particularly individuals unfamiliar with the legal limits of foreign government authority in Singapore
redFlags: Caller claims your bank account is linked to criminal activities
Call is transferred to a second 'foreign official' mid-conversation
Request to transfer money to police, bank accounts, or mobile numbers linked to bank accounts
Request for SingPass, banking credentials, or CPF-related information
Instruction to click links leading to bank websites
Instruction to install apps from outside official app stores
Request to meet in person to hand over cash for bail or investigation
Caller cites your personal information (name, NRIC) to appear legitimate
Pressure to stage a fake abduction for ransom extraction from family
legitimacyContrast: Real Singapore Police Force officers will never ask you over the phone or via text to transfer money to police or bank accounts, request banking or SingPass details, ask you to click links to bank websites, or instruct you to install apps outside official app stores. Foreign governments have no legal powers to conduct investigations in Singapore or direct anyone in Singapore to assist in their investigations. Legitimate law enforcement will not demand cash handovers or bail payments in person from members of the public.
preventionTips: Do not automatically trust a caller just because they know your personal information such as your name or NRIC number
Remember that police officers will never ask you to transfer money, provide SingPass or banking credentials, click bank links, or install unofficial apps over the phone or via text
Foreign governments have no legal authority to conduct investigations in Singapore; hang up immediately if a 'foreign official' makes such demands
Call the Police immediately if an alleged foreign police officer requests to meet you in person in Singapore to pay money or sign documents for bail or investigation
If unsure, stay calm and call the 24/7 ScamShield Helpline at 1799 to verify
Never hand over cash in person to anyone claiming to be a government official without independent verification
Do not stage or participate in any fake abduction scenario regardless of threats made

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify suspicious calls or report scams
Call 999 or visit the nearest police post to report immediately if a foreign police officer requests an in-person meeting for payment or document signing
Report scam calls and messages via the ScamShield app

name: Government Agency Impersonation Phishing (Elections, CDC & GST Vouchers)
sampleTexts: Dear resident, the Elections Department requires you to verify your voting status. Please click the link to confirm: http://elections-verify-sg.com/status
Your CDC voucher redemption is pending. Log in to confirm your details at: http://cdcvouchers-gov-sg.net/redeem
Important: Your GST Voucher disbursement requires verification. Please update your Singpass details here: http://gst-voucher-sg.info/verify
Action required: Verify your voting eligibility with the Elections Department. Failure to do so may affect your voter registration. Click: http://spf-elections-sg.com/verify
description: Scammers send unsolicited fake text messages or emails via SMS, WhatsApp, Telegram, or email impersonating Singapore government agencies such as the Elections Department. Messages lure victims with prompts to verify their voting status or claim CDC and GST vouchers. Embedded phishing links redirect victims to convincing fake government websites where personal and banking credentials — including bank login details, credit card information, Singpass credentials, and One-Time PINs — are harvested. Stolen credentials are then used to conduct unauthorised bank transactions and/or take over victims' messaging accounts. Scammers may also cite victims' personal information such as name or NRIC number to appear legitimate.
targetDemographic: General Public, particularly Singapore residents eligible for CDC/GST vouchers and registered voters
redFlags: Unsolicited SMS, WhatsApp, Telegram message or email from a supposed government agency
Message contains a clickable link directing to a website not ending in .gov.sg
Request to verify voting status or claim vouchers via a link
Request for Singpass login credentials, bank details, credit card information, or OTPs
Scammer cites your personal information (name, NRIC) to appear legitimate
Urgency or threat of consequences if action is not taken immediately
SMS Sender ID does not show gov.sg
legitimacyContrast: All legitimate Singapore government SMSes are sent from the gov.sg SMS Sender ID. All genuine government websites and email addresses contain '.gov.sg' in the URL or email address. Government officials will NEVER ask victims to transfer money or disclose personal or banking details over a phone call or via unsolicited messages. Legitimate government agencies do not send unsolicited links requesting credential verification.
preventionTips: Never click on links from unsolicited text messages or emails, regardless of the apparent sender
Verify that any government SMS originates from the gov.sg Sender ID
Confirm that government websites and email addresses contain '.gov.sg' in the URL or address
Check suspicious text messages, phone numbers, and website links using the ScamShield app
Do not trust someone solely because they possess your personal information such as your name or NRIC number
Remember that government officials will never ask you to transfer money or disclose personal or banking details over a phone call
Call the 24/7 ScamShield Helpline at 1799 if you are unsure whether a message is a scam

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify suspicious messages or report scams
Check suspicious phone numbers, text messages, and website links via the ScamShield app
Lodge a police report at the nearest Neighbourhood Police Centre or online at www.police.gov.sg

name: Concert Ticket Resale Scam (Lady Gaga / High-Demand Events)
sampleTexts: Hi! I have 2 extra Lady Gaga concert tickets (Cat 1) that I can't use. Selling at face value. Can transfer to your Ticketmaster account once payment is made. Have receipt as proof. DM me on Telegram!

Selling Lady Gaga Singapore tickets! Bought 4 but only need 2. Will send you screenshot of purchase confirmation. Payment via PayNow, transfer to your Ticketmaster account guaranteed. Contact me on Xiaohongshu.
LF: Lady Gaga tix? I have spares! Legit seller, can show NRIC and receipt. Ticketmaster transfer done after payment. Grab fast before they're gone! DM on X (Twitter).
description: Scammers exploit high demand for sold-out or popular concerts (e.g. Lady Gaga) by advertising resale tickets on messaging and social media platforms such as Telegram, X (Twitter), and Xiaohongshu. To appear legitimate, they provide fabricated screenshots of purchase confirmations or receipts, and may present stolen NRIC images as identity proof. They promise to transfer tickets to the victim's Ticketmaster account upon payment. Victims only discover the fraud after transferring money or when they are denied entry at the concert venue. Notably, Ticketmaster had disabled the ticket transfer function for the Lady Gaga concert, making any such promise a false claim.
targetDemographic: Concert fans, particularly those who failed to secure tickets through official channels due to high demand; general public seeking deals during seasonal sales or major events
redFlags: Seller claims to transfer tickets directly to your Ticketmaster account (transfer function was disabled for Lady Gaga concert)
Transaction conducted via informal messaging or social media platforms (Telegram, X, Xiaohongshu) rather than authorised platforms
Seller provides screenshots of receipts or purchases as proof of legitimacy
Seller shares NRIC or identity documents as trust-building measure (these are often stolen)
Urgency tactics used to pressure quick payment
Tickets offered at face value or below market price to attract buyers
No official platform listing or verifiable seller account
legitimacyContrast: Authorised ticket platforms (Ticketmaster, Klook, KrisFlyer) sell tickets directly through their official websites and apps. Ticketmaster explicitly disabled the ticket transfer function for the Lady Gaga concert. Legitimate platforms do not conduct sales via Telegram, X, or Xiaohongshu DMs, and will never ask buyers to pay individuals directly via PayNow or bank transfer for ticket transfers.
estimatedLoss: S$17,500,000 (total e-commerce scam losses in 2024, of which concert tickets were the top item)
preventionTips: Purchase concert tickets only from authorised platforms: Ticketmaster, Klook, and KrisFlyer
Be aware that Ticketmaster disabled the ticket transfer function for the Lady Gaga concert — any seller claiming to transfer tickets to your account is lying
Do not buy resale tickets from individuals on Telegram, X, or Xiaohongshu
Do not accept screenshots of receipts or NRIC images as proof of legitimacy — these can be fabricated or stolen
Understand that individuals with resale tickets will be denied entry at the venue with no refund
If unsure, call the 24/7 ScamShield Helpline at 1799 before making any payment
Verify any ticket-related claims directly with the official platform's customer service

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify or report suspected scams
Report scam messages or profiles via the ScamShield mobile app
Lodge a police report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness or at the nearest Neighbourhood Police Centre

name: Fake Investment Scheme (Multi-Channel Recruitment)
sampleTexts: Hi! I recently made S$8,000 in just 2 weeks using this investment platform. My friend introduced me to it and I've already withdrawn my first profits. Want me to add you to our investment group?
🚨 EXCLUSIVE INVESTMENT OPPORTUNITY 🚨 Join our private trading group and earn guaranteed returns! See what others are saying: [fake testimonials]. Limited slots available. DM us now!
Congratulations! Your initial investment of S$500 has grown to S$1,200. Reinvest now to maximise your returns! Click here to view your portfolio: [fake investment app link]
I saw [celebrity name] endorsing this investment platform and decided to try it. I've already made S$3,000 profit this month. Let me add you to the group chat!
description: Scammers recruit victims through recommendations from online-only acquaintances never met in person, random investment chat groups, or fake online advertisements. They entice victims to invest increasing sums by: (1) creating fake investment websites or apps displaying fabricated profits, (2) misappropriating images of public figures to create false endorsements, (3) presenting fake testimonials of others making profits, and (4) sending victims small initial 'profits' to convince them the scheme is genuine. After victims invest larger amounts of money or cryptocurrencies, they experience difficulties withdrawing their earnings, at which point the scammers may demand additional fees or simply disappear. Over S$24.5 million was lost to such investment scams since March 2025.
targetDemographic: General Public, particularly individuals active on social media and messaging platforms who may be susceptible to online relationship-building and investment promises
redFlags: Investment opportunity recommended by someone you have never met in person
Added to a random investment chat group by an unknown number
Fake advertisements encountered online promoting investment schemes
Fake investment websites or apps showing inflated or fabricated profits
Use of public figures' images for false endorsements
Fake testimonials claiming others have made large profits
Small initial 'profits' sent to build trust before requesting larger investments
Difficulties or inability to withdraw earnings after investing larger amounts
Investment entity or person not regulated by MAS
Social media posts by companies not listed on MAS register
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate investment entities in Singapore are regulated by the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) and are listed on the MAS website. Real licensed financial entities do not recruit clients through unsolicited chat groups, online-only relationships, or unverified social media advertisements. They do not use celebrity images without authorisation, and clients can withdraw funds without artificial barriers. MAS provides a public register of licensed financial entities and the MoneySense website for investment-related resources. MAS explicitly states it cannot help victims recover assets lost to unregulated entities.
preventionTips: Deal only with investment entities or persons regulated by the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS)
Check the MAS website for the list of licensed financial entities before investing
Visit the MoneySense website for investment-related resources and guidance
Block and report any account or phone number that recommends investments from someone you have never met in person
Report and leave any chat group you were added to by an unknown number
Be sceptical of investment opportunities promoted via social media by unregulated companies
Do not be convinced by small initial 'profits' — these are a common tactic to build false trust
Verify celebrity endorsements directly through official channels before trusting investment claims
If unsure whether something is a scam, call the 24/7 ScamShield Helpline at 1799

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify or report suspected scams
Block and report scam accounts or phone numbers via the ScamShield app
Check https://www.mas.gov.sg for the list of licensed financial entities
Visit https://www.moneysense.gov.sg for investment-related resources
Report and leave any suspicious investment chat group you were added to by an unknown number

Do not click on links embedded in unsolicited emails about expired tokens
Call POSB directly using the phone number on their official website to verify

sampleTexts: 🎉 [Brand Name] MEGA SALE! Get 80% off all products this weekend only! Click here to claim your exclusive deal before it expires: http://brandname-promo-sg.com/claim
Limited time offer from [Local Company]! Free gift with every purchase above $50. Click the link in our bio to shop now and enjoy exclusive member discounts!
description: Scammers create fake advertisements on social media platforms impersonating legitimate local companies and offering enticing promotions. Victims who click on these ads are directed to phishing sites or fraudulent storefronts designed to steal payment information or personal data.

redFlags: Advertisement offers unusually large discounts or promotions

name: Government Officials Impersonation Scam (GOIS)
sampleTexts: This is Officer Tan from the Singapore Police Force. We have detected suspicious transactions linked to your NRIC. You are required to transfer your funds to a designated safety account immediately to assist with our investigation. Failure to comply may result in arrest.

Hello, I am calling from the Monetary Authority of Singapore. Your bank account has been flagged for money laundering activities. To prove your innocence, you must exchange your savings for gold bars and hand them over to our officer. Do not inform anyone or you will be charged.

This is the Ministry of Manpower. We have evidence that your identity has been used in criminal activities. Please download this app [link] to cooperate with our investigation and provide your bank login details for verification.
description: Scammers impersonate government officials from the Singapore Police Force (SPF), Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS), or Ministry of Manpower (MOM). They call victims and falsely accuse them of involvement in criminal activities. Victims are then pressured to transfer money to so-called 'safety' bank accounts, hand over jewellery, or exchange money for gold bars under the pretexts of 'proving their innocence' or cooperating with an 'investigation'. Scammers may also instruct victims to download apps from third-party websites or provide bank login credentials. To appear credible, scammers cite victims' personal information such as full name or NRIC number. Calls may be spoofed to appear as if they originate from official government agencies, and scammers may claim to transfer the call to the police.
targetDemographic: General Public, particularly individuals unfamiliar with government procedures or those who may be intimidated by authority figures
redFlags: Caller claims to be a government official from SPF, MAS, or MOM
Accusation of involvement in criminal activities over the phone
Request to transfer money to a 'safety' bank account
Request to hand over jewellery or exchange money for gold bars
Request to download an app from a third-party (non-official) website
Request to provide bank login credentials over the phone
Caller claims to transfer you to the police
Caller cites your personal information (name, NRIC) to appear legitimate
Urgency and threats of arrest or legal consequences if you do not comply
SMS received from a sender ID that is not gov.sg
legitimacyContrast: Real Singapore government officials will never call to demand money transfers, request bank login details over the phone, ask victims to exchange money for gold bars or surrender jewellery, or instruct individuals to download apps from third-party websites. Legitimate government SMS communications are sent exclusively from the official gov.sg SMS Sender ID. The ScamShield Helpline (1799) is available 24/7 for the public to verify suspicious calls or messages.
preventionTips: Government officials will never ask you to transfer money or disclose bank login details over a phone call — hang up immediately.
Do not trust a caller solely because they know your personal information such as your name or NRIC number.
Never download apps from third-party websites at the request of an unsolicited caller.
Never hand over jewellery or exchange money for gold bars at the request of someone claiming to be a government official.

Report suspicious calls or messages to the police via the ScamShield app or by calling 999.

The use of legitimate local accounts and phone numbers also enhances the perceived credibility of scams, increasing the success rate of deceiving victims. description: As part of job scam operations, scammers request victims to share their Singpass login credentials under the guise of identity verification or account registration during the job application process.
