If a job application requests Singpass credentials, terminate contact immediately and report the incident
Use Singpass MyInfo only through the official Singpass app or website where you personally authorise data sharing
Call the 24/7 ScamShield Helpline at 1799 if you have been asked to share your Singpass details
Change your Singpass password immediately if you have already shared your credentials

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to report credential harvesting attempts
Check suspicious messages, phone numbers, and website links via the ScamShield app
Contact Singpass helpdesk immediately at 6335-3533 if credentials have been compromised
Lodge a police report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness or at the nearest Neighbourhood Police Centre

name: Fake School Fees Payment Request via Impersonated Educational Institution Email
sampleTexts: Dear Student, our records indicate that you have an outstanding school fee balance of S$1,250.00. Failure to make payment within 24 hours will result in disciplinary action and suspension of your student account. Please click the link below or scan the QR code to proceed with payment immediately: [LINK]

URGENT: This is a reminder from the Finance Office regarding your unpaid tuition fees. Please transfer the outstanding amount to the new bank account provided below. Failure to comply will result in academic consequences. Contact finance@sch00l-sg.edu for queries.
description: Scammers impersonate educational institutions by using either compromised student email accounts or fake email domains closely resembling official institutional addresses. They send emails demanding urgent payment of outstanding school or tuition fees, threatening disciplinary action if payment is not made promptly. Victims are directed to use clickable links or QR codes that lead to fake bank websites designed to steal banking credentials.
targetDemographic: Students, parents, and guardians associated with educational institutions in Singapore
redFlags: Email requests urgent payment of outstanding school or tuition fees
Threats of disciplinary action if payment is not made immediately
Email domain closely resembles but does not exactly match the official institutional address
Email originates from a compromised student email account rather than an official finance or admin address
Payment instructions include new or suddenly changed bank account details
Clickable links or QR codes provided for payment that may redirect to fake bank websites
Pressure to act quickly without time to verify
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate educational institutions in Singapore will communicate fee payment instructions through official and verified channels, will not threaten immediate disciplinary action via email for outstanding fees, will not suddenly change bank account details without formal prior notice, and will never request payment via unverified links or QR codes sent by email. Students and parents can always verify payment instructions by calling the institution directly using contact details from the official school website.
preventionTips: Always verify payment instructions directly with the educational institution using official contact details before making any payment
Be alert to any new or sudden changes in payment instructions or bank account numbers
Never use clickable links or QR codes provided in unsolicited emails to make payments or transfers
Check the sender's email domain carefully for subtle misspellings or variations from the official institutional address
Call the 24/7 ScamShield Helpline at 1799 if you are unsure whether a request is legitimate
Report suspicious emails to the institution's IT or security team immediately

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify or report the scam
Report the scam via the ScamShield mobile application
Lodge a Police report at the nearest Neighbourhood Police Centre or online at www.police.gov.sg

name: K-Pop Merchandise E-Commerce Scam
sampleTexts: Hi! I have G-Dragon official merch available for pre-order at a great price. Send me a PayNow deposit of $50 to secure your item before stocks run out! 🎶
Selling authentic K-pop merchandise from G-Dragon's latest tour. Limited stock! Transfer deposit via PayNow to reserve. DM me to move to WhatsApp for faster deal 😊
Pre-order G-Dragon photobook – only $30! Pay 50% deposit via bank transfer now to lock in your order. Huge discount for quick transactions only!
description: Scammers pose as sellers of K-pop merchandise (e.g., G-Dragon items) on platforms such as Carousell. After a victim expresses interest, the scammer persuades them to pay an initial deposit via PayNow or bank transfer to 'secure' the item. The scammer may also move the conversation off the platform to avoid detection. Victims only discover the scam when the products are never delivered or the seller becomes uncontactable.
targetDemographic: K-pop fans, particularly younger consumers purchasing or pre-ordering merchandise from popular artists on second-hand or resale platforms
redFlags: Seller requests upfront deposit via PayNow or bank transfer before delivering goods
Seller moves conversation off the e-commerce platform (e.g., to WhatsApp or Telegram)
Large discounts offered in exchange for faster or immediate transactions
Seller becomes uncontactable after payment is made
No option for cash-on-delivery or platform-secured payment offered
Listing involves high-demand or limited-edition K-pop merchandise
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate sellers on reputable e-commerce platforms offer secure in-platform payment options and cash-on-delivery, allowing buyers to inspect goods before paying. They do not request off-platform communication or upfront bank transfers/PayNow deposits to 'reserve' items.
preventionTips: Always use the e-commerce platform's secure payment option or cash-on-delivery so you can inspect products before paying
Do not make any payment prior to receiving or inspecting the products
Be wary of large discounts offered to incentivise faster transactions
Only purchase K-pop merchandise from authorised sellers on online platforms or physical retail stores
Check the platform's transaction safety ratings at go.gov.sg/mhatsr to verify anti-scam measures
Do not move conversations off the platform at a seller's request
If unsure, call the 24/7 ScamShield Helpline at 1799

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify or report a scam
Report suspicious sellers or messages via the ScamShield app
Lodge a police report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness or at the nearest Neighbourhood Police Centre
Report the suspicious listing or seller directly through the platform's in-app reporting feature

name: Pokémon Trading Card Pre-Order E-Commerce Scam
sampleTexts: Hi! I have Pokémon card booster boxes available for pre-order at 30% off retail price. Limited slots only! Send me a PayNow deposit of $50 to secure your order now before stocks run out.
Selling rare Pokémon cards at huge discount for fast deal. Transfer $200 via PayNow to reserve. Will deliver once stock arrives next week!
Pre-order Pokémon Scarlet & Violet booster box at $80 (usual $120). Bank transfer to confirm slot. Serious buyers only, fast lobang!
description: Scammers post fraudulent pre-order listings for Pokémon trading cards on online platforms such as Carousell. When victims express interest, scammers persuade them to pay initial deposits via PayNow or direct bank transfers to secure the pre-order. Victims only discover they have been scammed when the products are never delivered or when the sellers become uncontactable. Since October 2025, at least S$958,000 has been lost to this scam variant.
targetDemographic: Pokémon card collectors, hobbyists, and general online shoppers seeking discounted or hard-to-find collectibles
redFlags: Pre-order listings on peer-to-peer platforms like Carousell for high-demand collectibles
Large discounts offered to incentivise faster transactions
Requests for advance payment or deposit via PayNow or direct bank transfer before goods are received
Seller becomes uncontactable after payment is made
Products never delivered after deposit is paid
Pressure to transact quickly to secure limited stock
legitimacyContrast: Authorised sellers of Pokémon trading cards operate through verified online platforms with buyer protection mechanisms or through physical retail stores where goods can be inspected before purchase. Legitimate sellers do not require direct bank transfers or PayNow deposits for unverified pre-orders, and they maintain contactable customer service channels.
preventionTips: Only purchase Pokémon trading cards from authorised sellers on reputable online platforms or physical retail stores.
Avoid making advance payments or direct bank transfers to sellers you have not verified.
Use secure payment options that offer buyer protection rather than PayNow or direct bank transfers.
Consider cash-on-delivery arrangements so you can inspect the product before making payment.
Be wary of large discounts offered to pressure faster transactions — this is a common scam red flag.
Verify seller reviews, ratings, and transaction history before committing to any purchase.
If in doubt, call the 24/7 ScamShield Helpline at 1799 before making any payment.

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify if a listing or seller is a scam
Report the scam listing or seller via the ScamShield app
File a report at www.police.gov.sg/iwitness or at the nearest Neighbourhood Police Centre

name: IRAS Tax Refund Phishing Email Scam
sampleTexts: Dear Taxpayer, IRAS has processed your tax refund of S$842.00. Please click the link below to verify your bank or card details to receive your refund within 3 business days: http://iras-refund-sg.com/verify
IRAS: You are eligible for a tax refund. Action required — submit your card details at the link provided to claim your refund: http://mytax-iras-sg.net/claim
description: Scammers impersonate the Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore (IRAS) by sending phishing emails claiming the recipient is owed a tax refund. The email contains a malicious link directing victims to a fake website designed to steal personal information and banking or card details. Victims typically discover the scam only after noticing unauthorised transactions on their accounts or when they verify directly with IRAS.
targetDemographic: Singapore taxpayers, General Public
redFlags: Email requests action to claim a tax refund via a link
Email asks for credit or debit card details to process a refund
Sender email address does not end with 'gov.sg'
Urgency to click a link to receive money
Refund directed to card rather than registered bank account or PayNow
legitimacyContrast: IRAS automatically credits tax refunds to taxpayers' bank accounts registered with IRAS or PayNow accounts linked to NRIC/FIN/UEN. No action is required from the taxpayer. IRAS does not process refunds to credit or debit cards. Official IRAS emails will come from addresses ending in 'gov.sg' and taxpayers should verify via the myTax Portal.
preventionTips: Do not click links in unsolicited emails claiming to be from IRAS
Verify tax refund status directly on IRAS myTax Portal at mytax.iras.gov.sg
Check that the sender's email address ends with '@iras.gov.sg' or another official 'gov.sg' domain
Remember that IRAS never processes refunds to credit or debit cards — no action is needed from you
Call the ScamShield Helpline at 1799 if unsure

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify or report
Report the phishing email or link via the app
Verify tax refund status directly at mytax.iras.gov.sg

name: LTA Outstanding Bill / Unpaid Vehicle Toll Phishing Scam
sampleTexts: LTA: You have an outstanding vehicle toll charge of S$38.50. Failure to pay within 48 hours may result in penalties. Pay now: http://lta-toll-sg.com/pay
LTA Notice: An unpaid bill of S$62.00 is linked to your vehicle. Click here to settle immediately to avoid suspension of your road tax: http://onemotor-lta.net/settle
description: Scammers impersonate Singapore's Land Transport Authority (LTA) by sending phishing SMS messages or emails claiming the recipient has outstanding bills or unpaid vehicle tolls. The message contains a malicious link leading to a fake website designed to steal personal and banking or card details. Victims discover the scam only after noticing unauthorised transactions or verifying with LTA directly.
targetDemographic: Singapore vehicle owners, General Public
redFlags: SMS or email claims you have an unpaid LTA toll or outstanding bill
Message contains a link to pay or verify details
SMS sender ID is not 'gov.sg'
Urgency or threat of penalty to pressure immediate action
Link does not direct to the official OneMotoring platform
legitimacyContrast: Legitimate LTA text messages are only sent via the official 'gov.sg' sender ID. Motorists should verify any outstanding bills directly on LTA's OneMotoring platform. LTA will not send SMSes from unofficial sender IDs or request payment via links in messages.
preventionTips: Do not click links in SMS messages or emails claiming to be from LTA
Verify outstanding bills directly on LTA's OneMotoring platform at onemotoring.lta.gov.sg
Check that any SMS from LTA uses the official 'gov.sg' sender ID
Do not provide banking or card details via links received in messages
Call the ScamShield Helpline at 1799 if unsure

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify or report
Report the suspicious SMS or link via the app
Verify vehicle-related bills directly at onemotoring.lta.gov.sg

name: Royal Malaysian Police (RMP) Impersonation Phishing Scam
sampleTexts: ROYAL MALAYSIA POLICE: You have an outstanding traffic summons of RM 450 linked to your vehicle. Failure to settle will result in arrest upon entry to Malaysia. Click here: http://rmp-summons.com/pay
RMP Notice: You are required to assist in an ongoing investigation. Please click the link to submit your personal details and avoid legal action: http://rmp-sg-ops.net/verify
description: Scammers impersonate the Royal Malaysian Police (RMP) by sending phishing emails or SMS messages to Singapore residents, claiming they have outstanding bills, unpaid vehicle tolls, or are required to assist in investigations. The messages contain malicious links leading to fake websites designed to steal personal and banking or card details. Overseas law enforcement agencies have no jurisdiction to conduct operations in Singapore, arrest anyone, or request public assistance in investigations without Singapore Government approval.
targetDemographic: Singapore residents who travel to Malaysia or own vehicles, General Public
redFlags: Message claims to be from a foreign law enforcement agency such as the Royal Malaysian Police
Claims of outstanding fines, summons, or requests to assist in investigations
Contains a link to submit personal or payment details
Threatens arrest or legal consequences to pressure action
Foreign police agencies contacting Singapore residents directly without Singapore Government involvement
legitimacyContrast: Overseas law enforcement agencies have no jurisdiction to conduct operations in Singapore, arrest anyone, or ask members of the public to assist in any form of investigations without the approval of the Singapore Government. Legitimate government SMSes use the 'gov.sg' sender ID. Any such contact should be treated as suspicious and verified with Singapore authorities.
preventionTips: Do not click links in emails or SMS messages claiming to be from foreign law enforcement agencies
Be aware that overseas law enforcement agencies have no jurisdiction in Singapore without Singapore Government approval
Do not provide personal or banking details in response to such messages
Verify any claims with Singapore Police Force (SPF) directly
Call the ScamShield Helpline at 1799 if unsure

reportingChannels.action: Call 1799 (24/7) to verify or report
Report the suspicious email or SMS via the app
Lodge a police report at www.police.gov.sg or call 999 for emergencies
